trigger: User felt Studio design was too heavy, needed help planning redesign direction
Color correction audit across Studio surfaces
Token system audit for sidebar color migration

description: Fade texture layers (reduce opacity) instead of removing them entirely

modifications: User directed to increase transparency rather than zero out textures

description: Remove Studio Pulse insights, stat tiles, and Recently Touched from workbench to eliminate scrollbar

modifications: First attempt removed too much (entire sections). User clarified: remove only Quiet insight type and detail text under each insight, keep other sections. Later separately removed Recently Touched.

description: Remove TRAVISGILBERT.ME subtitle, remove Collapse button, reduce Studio font from 54px to 42px
Replace dark Studio ground with main site's parchment canvas (DotGrid component swap, token alignment)

modifications: Initially matched main site colors exactly but user felt it didn't look as good without dark gradient. Pivoted to deep slate blue sidebars with cream text as contrast strategy.

description: Deep slate blue (#2C3E50) sidebars with cream text (#F0E6D2), teal/terracotta/gold section labels for contrast

modifications: User enthusiastically approved the slate blue direction

description: Section label colors (#7A3A1A, #1E4A52, #8A6A2A) designed for parchment sidebar were invisible on slate blue. Brightened to #E08A4A, #6AD0E0, #E8C060
Add underlines to sidebar section labels via border-bottom: 1px solid currentColor
Change NOW card from green typeColor to teal, add inset box-shadow for recessed feel

When migrating a surface from dark to light (or vice versa), hardcoded color values in JS data structures (not just CSS) need auditing. Section label colors, content type colors, and badge colors may all need brightness adjustment for the new background.

User communication about element selection scope is ambiguous: selecting individual items within a section can mean 'remove these items' or 'remove the entire parent section containing them.' Naming the section or stating what should stay is clearer than selecting children.
Deep slate blue (#2C3E50 family) provides strong contrast against warm parchment (#F5E6D2) without the heaviness of near-black. It reads as professional tooling rather than dark-mode aesthetic.
Texture layers (grain, grid lines, dot fields) should be faded rather than removed when a design feels heavy. opacity: 0.2-0.4 preserves character while reducing visual weight.
CSS color-mix() with CSS custom properties (var(--token)) works correctly in modern browsers, enabling studioMix() to accept both hex values and CSS variables as typeColor.

Matching the main site's exact color tokens (#F5E6D2 paper) produced a technically correct but aesthetically flat result in the Studio. The Studio needed its own contrast strategy (slate sidebars) rather than pixel-perfect color matching.
